questions: Locate the 'UNIX Tutorial for Beginners' authored by M. Stonebank (originally published around October 2001). Identify the specific version of the Creative Commons license linked to this tutorial. Based on the legal deed for that specific license version, detail the two categories of actions users are 'free to' perform and the three specific 'terms' under which these freedoms are granted.

rounds.question: Locate the 'UNIX Tutorial for Beginners' authored by M. Stonebank (originally published around October 2001). Identify the specific version of the Creative Commons license linked to this tutorial. Based on the legal deed for that specific license version, detail the two categories of actions users are 'free to' perform and the three specific 'terms' under which these freedoms are granted.
checklist_width: Freedom: Share (copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format)
Freedom: Adapt (remix, transform, and build upon the material)
Term: Attribution (give appropriate credit, provide a link to the license, and indicate if changes were made)
Term: NonCommercial (may not use the material for commercial purposes)
Term: ShareAlike (must distribute contributions under the same license as the original)
checklist_depth: Target Entity: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0)
Logic Proof: The tutorial by M. Stonebank (Source A) links to the CC license, and the text (Source B) identifies the canonical URL as version 2.0.
rounds.rationale: The question requires Deep reasoning to link the specific tutorial (M. Stonebank, 2001) to the correct license version (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0), distinguishing it from newer versions mentioned in the license deed's header. It requires Wide aggregation to extract the specific structure of permissions (Freedoms) and conditions (Terms) from the license deed.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Notice This is an older version of this license. Compared to previous versions, the 4.0 versions of all CC licenses are more user-friendly and more internationally robust . If
rounds.answer_a:
The UNIX Tutorial for Beginners by M. Stonebank, originally published in October 2001, is distributed under the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales License [1][2].

According to the legal deed for this specific Creative Commons license version, users are granted two categories of freedoms [3][4]:

**Free to:**
1. **Share** - Users can copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format
2. **Adapt** - Users can remix, transform, and build upon the material

These freedoms are granted under three specific terms [3][4]:

**Terms:**
1. **Attribution** - Users must give appropriate credit to the original author, provide a link to the license, and indicate if changes were made. The attribution must be done in a reasonable manner, but not in any way that suggests the licensor endorses the user or their use.

2. **NonCommercial** - Users may not use the material for commercial purposes. This means the material cannot be used primarily for commercial advantage or monetary compensation.

3. **ShareAlike** - If users remix, transform, or build upon the material, they must distribute their contributions under the same license as the original. This ensures that derivative works remain under the same terms, maintaining the open nature of the content.

This particular license version (2.0 UK: England & Wales) was one of the earlier Creative Commons licenses, later superseded by international versions. The tutorial has been widely used in academic settings and remains a valuable resource for Unix beginners, with its open licensing ensuring continued availability and adaptation by the educational community.

## References

[1] http://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/
[2] https://www.cs.columbia.edu/~hgs/teaching/isp/slides/unix.pdf
[3] https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/legalcode
[4] https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/
rounds.answer_b:
The “UNIX Tutorial for Beginners” by M. Stonebank carries the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 Unported licence (CC BY-NC-SA 3.0) [1].

Under the CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 legal deed, readers are explicitly free to do two things:

1. Share — copy and redistribute the tutorial in any medium or format.  
2. Adapt — remix, transform and build upon the tutorial.

These freedoms are irrevocable so long as three licence terms are observed [2]:

1. Attribution — you must give appropriate credit, provide a link to the licence and indicate if changes were made.  
2. NonCommercial — you may not use the material for commercial purposes.  
3. ShareAlike — if you remix, transform or build upon the material, you must distribute your contributions under the same licence as the original.

Complying with these conditions enables educators, students and hobbyists to circulate or customise the tutorial while ensuring that Michael Stonebank receives credit, that no commercial exploitation occurs without separate permission and that any derivative works remain equally open. ## References 1. M. Stonebank, “UNIX Tutorial for Beginners.” University of Surrey, licence statement at foot of index page (accessed 15 Dec 2025). 2. Creative Commons, “Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 Unported (CC BY-NC-SA 3.0) – Legal Code & Deed.” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/3.0/
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent A correctly identified the specific license version (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK) used by the M. Stonebank tutorial. Agent B incorrectly identified the license as version 3.0. Since the query explicitly asked for the *specific version* and the details based on that version, Agent B's failure is significant. Agent A also provided better formatting and citation density.
